video_script.title: The Fire That Never Reaches Denver Is Already in Your Lungs
video_script.hook: Morning AQI checks have become as routine as weather forecasts in north Denver, where families now duct-tape box fans to furnace filters every August.
body_slides: September 5th. Denver's air ranked worst in America.
No evacuation orders. No fires for hundreds of miles.
Families duct-tape box fans to furnace filters. Again.
video_script.voiceover_text: Denver had America's worst air September 5th. No fires burned for hundreds of miles. The smoke choking your city today started in forests you'll never see. September 5th. Denver's air ranked worst in America. No evacuation orders. No fires for hundreds of miles. Families duct-tape box fans to furnace filters. Again. The fire in your lungs started in someone else's forest. That's The Docket. Follow for more stories like this.
video_script.cta:
Follow @docketclimate for more
Climate news · Adaptation · Speculation
Link in bio
video_script.voiceover_cta: That's The Docket. Follow for more stories like this.
image_prompts: Wide shot of Denver skyline shrouded in hazy orange smoke, downtown buildings barely visible through thick atmospheric pollution, golden hour lighting, portrait orientation
Close-up of hands pressing duct tape around edges of a box fan with white furnace filter attached, kitchen counter background, natural indoor lighting, shallow depth of field
Smartphone screen showing purple AQI reading of 287, finger hovering over refresh button, morning sunlight streaming through sealed window blinds in background

Atmospheric documentary photograph visualizing: No evacuation orders. No fires for hundreds of miles.. Photojournalistic style, natural lighting, cinematic composition, 9:16 portrait, no text or UI elements.

Atmospheric documentary photograph visualizing: Families duct-tape box fans to furnace filters. Again.. Photojournalistic style, natural lighting, cinematic composition, 9:16 portrait, no text or UI elements.

items.text:

🔗 Link in bio

video_script.title: The Woman Who Sells Phoenix Back Its Own Desert
video_script.hook: Tier 3 water restrictions in Phoenix have residents tracking usage like their parents tracked gas prices.
body_slides: Desiree's nursery: rows of barrel cactus, palo verde, ocotillo.
Water meters downtown read Tier 3. Four years running.
Her customers arrive with dead grass photos on phones.
video_script.voiceover_text: Phoenix water restrictions have hit Tier 3 four years running. Traditional landscaping is now functionally illegal. Desiree Quintero sells desert plants back to a city built on borrowed water. Desiree's nursery: rows of barrel cactus, palo verde, ocotillo. Water meters downtown read Tier 3. Four years running. Her customers arrive with dead grass photos on phones. Phoenix landscaping its own obituary. More at The Docket. Follow to keep seeing these.
video_script.cta:
Follow @docketclimate for more
Climate news · Adaptation · Speculation
Link in bio
video_script.voiceover_cta: More at The Docket. Follow to keep seeing these.
image_prompts: Desert plant nursery at golden hour, rows of barrel cacti and ocotillo against weathered concrete, dramatic shadows between plants, portrait orientation
Close-up of a residential water meter showing Tier 3 restriction warning, cracked pavement around base, harsh midday Arizona sun, documentary style
Woman's weathered hands holding smartphone displaying dead brown lawn photo, desert plants blurred in background, natural lighting
Expensive-looking palo verde saplings with price tags in foreground, vast empty subdivision lots stretching behind, late afternoon light

Atmospheric documentary photograph visualizing: Her customers arrive with dead grass photos on phones.. Photojournalistic style, natural lighting, cinematic composition, 9:16 portrait, no text or UI elements.
